Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty loose this semester. I'd usually carve out about 16 hours a week, but it wasn't like a strict schedule or anything. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time making diagrams and color-coded notes rather than just reading. With seven online courses going on, I had to juggle quite a bit, so I'd often batch similar tasks together when I could focus.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due and what else was going on in my life. I have extracurriculars that take up time, so some weeks I'd be cramming everything into a few intense sessions. Other weeks, when things were calmer, I'd spread it out more evenly. Deadlines were really the main driver of when I'd buckle down.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or needing to check if there was new material posted. Sometimes I'd log in because I got a notification about a discussion post or a grade being released. I wasn't the type to just browse the LMS for fun—there had to be a specific reason pulling me in.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. I never got into a rhythm where checking the LMS felt automatic. I'd log in about once a week on average, mostly when I remembered or when something urgent came up. It always felt more like a chore than a habit, even though I knew I should probably check it more often.
Q5: The video content was definitely the most useful for me—I watched over 200 minutes worth because I really absorb things better visually. Seeing concepts demonstrated or explained on screen sticks with me way more than reading text. The gamified tasks were also kind of fun and kept me engaged in a way that regular assignments didn't.
Q6: I tended to skip a lot of the reading materials and some of the discussion forums. Reading walls of text just doesn't work well for how I learn, so I'd skim them at best. The forums felt like busywork sometimes, even though I did post when I had to. I just prioritized what I knew would actually help me understand the material.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was going to directly help me on assignments or exams. If I could see a clear connection between doing something and improving my grade, I was in. The gamified elements also made things feel more worthwhile because there was that sense of achievement and progress.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt way easier to skip. If something was required, I'd make sure to do it even if I wasn't feeling motivated. But optional stuff? I'd tell myself I'd get to it later and then never did. There's just so much competing for my attention that optional things fall off the radar.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download slides and videos and then study from my tablet or laptop offline. I also used external YouTube videos and some apps that helped me create visual study aids. A lot of my actual learning happened with my own notes spread out on my desk, not logged into anything.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be working through problems in a notebook or reviewing printed diagrams, and none of that showed up anywhere online. I'd also study with friends sometimes, quizzing each other or explaining concepts, which was super helpful but completely invisible to the LMS. My best learning often happened away from the screen.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, maybe take some screenshots or notes, and then review those offline repeatedly. Same with PDFs—I'd download them and annotate them on my tablet. So my one login or one view didn't reflect how many times I actually engaged with that material.
Q12: There were definitely stretches where my anxiety spiked and it got really hard to focus. Even though I'd describe my stress as generally low, my anxiety can get pretty intense and it makes everything feel overwhelming. During those times, I'd avoid the course because even opening the LMS felt like too much.
Q13: Competing responsibilities were a big factor. Between extracurriculars and managing seven courses, something always had to give. When I was tired or confused about material, I'd procrastinate on the LMS stuff because it felt easier to tackle things I already understood. Fatigue especially made me less likely to engage with anything demanding.
Q14: Oh definitely. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be thinking about other things I needed to do or just zoning out because I was anxious. Those sessions looked like engagement but I wasn't really absorbing anything meaningful.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even when I knew they mattered. Part of it was anxiety—starting something felt harder than avoiding it. I'd tell myself I worked better under pressure, which was partly true but also just an excuse. Some things I avoided because they seemed confusing and I didn't want to deal with that feeling.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main thing that got me to use the LMS consistently. I'd ramp up my activity significantly in the days before something was due. Without a deadline looming, I'd drift and focus on other things. It's not ideal, but that pressure was what kept me on track.
Q17: Feedback did motivate me when it was positive—seeing a good score on an assignment made me want to keep that momentum. But if feedback was critical or I didn't do well, it sometimes made me avoid the course for a bit because of the anxiety. I'd need time to process before jumping back in.
Q18: The structure was okay, but with my low attendance rate, I wasn't always sure what was happening when. The gamified tasks helped because they gave me clear goals and a sense of progression. But overall, I sometimes felt lost about the bigger picture of where I should be focusing my energy.
Q19: The gamification definitely made engagement easier—it gave me small wins that kept me going. But the sheer volume of content across all my courses made it harder to stay on top of everything. Clearer visual roadmaps or progress trackers would have helped someone like me who learns visually.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to figure things out but struggling to actually complete work. Maybe they're anxious about submitting something imperfect, or they're confused and keep revisiting materials without making progress. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. I passed the course and did well on assignments, but my LMS activity was pretty minimal. I did a ton of studying offline with my own materials and methods. The LMS just doesn't capture all the ways I actually learned and prepared.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or understanding anything. They miss all the offline work, the conversations with classmates, the mental effort of processing information. It's like looking at a shadow of what's really happening.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually thinking about the material and trying to understand it, not just going through the motions. It's when I care about learning something, not just passing. For me, engagement is active—making connections, asking questions in my head, applying concepts to things I already know.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much anxiety I dealt with or how that affected my engagement. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed notes and diagrams, or the study sessions with friends. It also can't show that even when I was happy and feeling good, I was still working hard in ways that weren't logged anywhere.
